summary: Martin Fowler reflects on his choice to use 'enterprise application' as terminology for a specific class of software systems—those dealing with persistent data, concurrent access, complex integrations, and business logic. He identifies key characteristics: heavy reliance on databases, concurrent multi-user access, numerous UI screens, integration with legacy systems across decades of technology, conceptual dissonance between systems, and complex business rules that defy logical simplification. While acknowledging the term has acquired unfortunate connotations associating it with large corporations rather than the technical characteristics he intended, he concludes it remains the best available terminology.
key_points: Enterprise applications are defined by technical characteristics (persistent data, concurrency, integration needs) rather than company size
Business logic in enterprise applications is inherently 'illogical'—accumulated special cases from thousands of sales deals that can't be simplified
Integration with other systems spanning decades of technology creates irreducible complexity, with each rationalization attempt adding more layers
The term 'enterprise' has unfortunately become associated with large corporations and even used as an insult in some communities like Ruby on Rails
Conceptual dissonance between applications means the same entity (like 'customer') has different meanings across different organizational contexts
money_quotes: This data will usually be longer lasting and more valuable than the applications that process it.
Despite high levels of concurrency, many enterprise application developers don't think much about critical regions, race conditions and other elements of classic concurrent programming.
Every so often there will be an attempt to rationalize all this communication technology, but they never entirely succeed leaving behind more complexity in their wake.
If you want to change them you need sixty-seven meetings and three vice-presidents retiring.
Their insanity derives from a good reason, each one is a case where salesman could close a particular deal by offering some special one-off condition.
Big systems make a lot of noise when they go wrong, yet the cumulative effect of small systems can have a surprising effect on an enterprise's health.
Just don't tell DHH I said so or he'll turn me into a hood ornament.
key_insight: Enterprise applications are defined not by the size of the company using them but by their technical characteristics: persistent data, concurrent access, complex integrations, and business rules that accumulate irrationality for good commercial reasons.
